coredata.dc:title: A self-calibrating ratiometric fluorescence sensor with photonic crystal-based signal amplification for the detection of tetracycline in food
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Licheng
Yang, Yi
Jiang, Xiaowen
Li, Yijun
He, Xiwen
Chen, Langxing
Zhang, Yukui
coredata.dc:description: A dual-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on photonic crystals (PCs) was developed to detect tetracycline (TC) in food. PC was fabricated via self-assembly of carbon dots (CDs)–loaded SiO2 nanoparticles. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and copper ions (Cu2+) were then adsorbed onto the PC for sensor fabrication. The fluorescence of AuNCs was amplified by the PC with an enhancement ratio of 7.6, providing higher sensitivity. The fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched by Cu2+, whereas that of CDs remained unchanged as an internal reference. TC restored the fluorescence of AuNCs owing to its complexation with Cu2+, resulting in a change in the fluorescence intensity ratio. The sensor exhibited a good linear relationship with TC concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 10 μM, with a detection limit of 34 nM. Furthermore, the sensor was applied for TC detection in food with satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviations, revealing great potential in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photonic crystal
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Gold nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Tetracycline detection
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SiO2 NPs and SiO2@CDs NPs
Ratiometric fluorescence assay of TC detection
Feasibility and sensing mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Selectivity and anti-interference properties of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor toward TC
Determination of TC in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074067).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor and ratiometric fluorescence detection of TC.

(A) TEM images of SiO2@CDs NPs. (Scale bar: 200 nm) (B) TEM images of SiO2@CDs NPs. (Scale bar: 10 nm) (C) TEM images of AuNCs. (Scale bar: 20 nm) (D) SEM image of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+. (Scale bar: 1 μm) (E) Reflectance spectra of PC (Inset: photographs of PC). (F) XPS spectra of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ (Blue), SiO2@CDs (Green) and AuNCs (Red).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PC@AuNCs with different concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 μM). (B) Scatter plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I610/I456) versus concentrations of Cu2+. (C) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs on the substrate of bare glass and PC. (D) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of Glass@AuNCs, PC@AuNCs, PC@AuNCs/Cu2+, and PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ + TC.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensors with different concentrations of TC (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 10 μM). (B) Scatter plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I610/I456) versus concentrations of TC. (C) Fluorescence photographs of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensors with different concentrations of TC under UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm. (D) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram against different concentrations of TC.

(A) The fluorescence intensity ratios of the sensor (I610/I456) with various substances (5 μM). (B) The fluorescence intensity ratios of the sensor (I610/I456) with the mixtures of TC and the substances (5 μM).
Detection of TC in milk and honey samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A self-calibrating ratiometric fluorescence sensor with photonic crystal-based signal amplification for the detection of tetracycline in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education (Nankai University), Tianjin 300071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education (Nankai University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education (Nankai University), Tianjin 300071, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116011, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on photonic crystals (PCs) was developed to detect tetracycline (TC) in food. PC was fabricated via self-assembly of carbon dots (CDs)–loaded SiO2 nanoparticles. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and copper ions (Cu2+) were then adsorbed onto the PC for sensor fabrication. The fluorescence of AuNCs was amplified by the PC with an enhancement ratio of 7.6, providing higher sensitivity. The fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched by Cu2+, whereas that of CDs remained unchanged as an internal reference. TC restored the fluorescence of AuNCs owing to its complexation with Cu2+, resulting in a change in the fluorescence intensity ratio. The sensor exhibited a good linear relationship with TC concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 10 μM, with a detection limit of 34 nM. Furthermore, the sensor was applied for TC detection in food with satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviations, revealing great potential in practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a broad-spectrum bacteriostatic antibiotic with excellent antibacterial properties and good oral absorption. It has been widely applied in human treatment and is used as a veterinary drug to promote animal growth and prevent animal diseases (Tang et al., 2022). However, the overuse of TC results in drug residues in environmental media and animal-origin foodstuffs such as milk and honey, which poses a serious threat to public health through food chain transmission, including allergic reactions, liver damage, and bacterial resistance to antibiotics (Martinez, 2009; Siddiqui et al., 2020). To ensure human health, many countries have established relevant regulations on the maximum residue limit (MRL) of TC in foodstuffs. For example, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the European Union (EU), and China have stipulated the MRL of TC in milk to be 676 nmol/L, 225 nmol/L, and 100 μg/kg (corresponding to 225 nmol/L), respectively. Therefore, developing an efficient, sensitive, and simple strategy for the detection of TC in foodstuffs is of great significance for food safety and human health.

Currently, numerous analytical methods are developed for detecting TC, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Babić, Ašperger, Mutavdžić, Horvat, & Kaštelan-Macan, 2006; Xu et al., 2017), high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) (Nakazawa et al., 1999; Nebot et al., 2014), enzyme-linked immunoassay (Pastor-Navarro, Morais, Maquieira, & Puchades, 2007), capillary electrophoresis (Diaz-Quiroz et al., 2018; Ibarra, Rodriguez, Miranda, Vega, & Barrado, 2011; Santos, Henriques, Duarte, & Esteves, 2007), and electrochemical methods (Abraham et al., 2020; Ramnani, Saucedo, & Mulchandani, 2016). Although most of these methods are efficient and accurate, their disadvantages include expensive analytical equipment, cumbersome detection processes, and time-consuming processes, limit their application in rapid and real-time online assays. Compared with the above methods, fluorometry has received much attention from researchers because of its superiorities involving high sensitivity, rapid response, simplicity, low cost, visual properties, and ease of operation (Khan et al., 2023). At present, various fluorescence sensors based on quantum dots (Liang et al., 2022; Thuy, Huy, Kumar, & Lee, 2021), carbon dots (Jia et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2021; Uriarte, Domini, & Garrido, 2019), metal–organic frameworks (Ameen, Qasim, Alhasan, Aziz, & Omer, 2023; Shi et al., 2023; Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022), and metal nanocluster (Khataee, Jalili, Dastborhan, Karimi, & Azar, 2020), among others, have been developed for TC detection. However, single emitting fluorescence sensors are susceptible to interference from factors such as the precision and stability of the instrument, the concentration of fluorescence probe, and fluctuations in test temperature (Lee, Kim, & Sessler, 2015). Fortunately, fluorescence sensors with dual-color fluorescence emissions based on a ratiometric strategy can effectively overcome these interferences because of its built-in self-calibration function (Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi, & Luque, 2019). Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence sensing platforms display higher sensitivity and detection accuracy than single emitting fluorescence sensors. In addition, ratiometric fluorescence sensors exhibit a distinct fluorescence color change caused by analytes, which is superior to the fluorescence intensity change observed in single emitting fluorescence sensors. Consequently, researchers have developed numerous ratiometric fluorescence sensors for monitoring various analytes in foodstuffs. Despite their potential, the further development of ratiometric fluorescence sensors in sensing applications faces several challenges. In practical scenarios, the residue concentration of analytes in foodstuffs is typically very low. Therefore, developing ratiometric fluorescence sensing platforms possessing high sensitivity for monitoring food contaminants remains a significant challenge.

Among fluorescent materials, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have garnered considerable attention in sensing and biomedicine owing to their properties, such as ultrasmall size, high stability, ease of surface functionalization, and low toxicity (Cui et al., 2023; Farkhani et al., 2024). However, compared with other developed fluorescence probes, AuNCs exhibit inferior luminous performance and relatively low quantum yield, leading to low sensitivity in detecting target substances, significantly limiting their sensing applications. Various approaches have been explored to improve the fluorescence performance of AuNCs, including the use of other ligands and methods to restrain the intramolecular movement of AuNCs (Havenridge & Aikens, 2023). However, these approaches have their own limitations, such as the need for organic solvents and complicated synthesis processes. Hence, it is still necessary to develop more efficient methods for enhancing the fluorescence of AuNCs.

Photonic crystals (PCs) are periodic structures formed by two or more types of dielectric materials with different dielectric constants. They hold significant application potential in the field of rapid sensing (Fenzl, Hirsch, & Wolfbeis, 2014). PCs possess specific optical properties, namely, the photonic bandgap (PBG) and slow light effect, which can prevent the propagation of light with specific frequencies in certain directions. At the edge of the PBG, the group velocity of light is notably reduced owing to the enhancement of the density of states (DOS), leading to enhanced light–matter interactions (Lis et al., 2023; Wang, Pinkse, Segerink, & Eijkel, 2021). Therefore, if the excitation or emission wavelength of fluorescence probes is consistent with the edge of the PBG, the fluorescence intensity of the probes can be effectively enhanced, thereby increasing detection sensitivity.

In this study, a self-calibrating ratiometric fluorescence sensor with PC-based signal amplification (PC@AuNCs/Cu2+) was prepared via a simple self-assembly and electrostatic adsorption process for TC detection in food samples. Carbon dots (CDs)–loaded SiO2 nanoparticles (SiO2@CDs NPs) were chosen as the unit cell of PC and served as a constant signal reference. The fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs NPs remains unchanged in the presence of Cu2+ and TC. The AuNCs adsorbed on the PC served as a response signal component, and their fluorescence was subsequently quenched by Cu2+ to complete the fabrication of the sensor. TC molecules could coordinate with Cu2+, leading to the fluorescence restoration of AuNCs and resulting in a variation in the fluorescence intensity ratio between AuNCs and CDs. Moreover, even a slight signal response generated by a low concentration of TC could be amplified because of the fluorescence enhancement effect of the PC, thereby improving the sensitivity of TC detection. In general, the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor combines the merits of dual-emission self-calibrating and PC-based signal amplification, demonstrating excellent accuracy and sensitivity in TC monitoring. This innovation is poised to offer a promising analysis strategy for practical applications.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4·3H2O was purchased from Meryer Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH), citric acid, tetracycline (TC), clarithromycin, chloramphenicol, kanamycin, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, demeclocycline, and doxycycline were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water produced by A.S. Watson Group, Ltd. (Hong Kong, China) was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a FEI Talos F200X G2 (FEI, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by Apero S Lovac SEM (FEI, USA). The elemental composition of PC was analyzed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with SEM. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were measured by FL-4600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescent lifetimes were measured by FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were performed by K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SiO2 NPs and SiO2@CDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ ratiometric fluorescent sensor was shown in Fig. 1 . To assemble the sensor, SiO2 NPs and SiO2@CDs NPs were firstly synthesized. Monodisperse SiO2 NPs were prepared by a published method with slight modifications (Pan et al., 2024). Briefly, a mixture solution A was prepared by 4 mL of NH3·H2O, 15 mL of ethanol and 25 mL of water; 4 mL of TEOS was dissolved into 45 mL of ethanol to prepare a mixture solution B. The solution A and B were mixed under stirring (1300 rpm) for 1 min, then the reaction worked under stirring (500 rpm) for 3 h to prepare the monodisperse SiO2 NPs. The SiO2 NPs were purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with ethanol to remove impurities. The obtained SiO2 NPs were dispersed in ethanol for later use.

To synthesize SiO2@CDs NPs, the SiO2-NH2 NPs were first prepared. In detail, 10 μL of APTES was added into 10 mL of SiO2 NPs suspension, and the mixture suspension was stirred for 6 h at room temperature. The SiO2-NH2 NPs were purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and successively washed three times with ethanol and water. The obtained SiO2-NH2 NPs were dispersed in 10 mL of water for later use. Then, the CDs were prepared on the surface of SiO2-NH2 NPs through hydrothermal reaction. Briefly, 3 mL of SiO2-NH2 NPs suspension was added into 7 mL of citric acid solution (15.6 mM) and the mixture was stirred for 2 min. After that, the above suspension was transferred to a teflon-lined autoclave chamber, which was sealed and maintained at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the SiO2@CDs NPs were purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and successively washed three times with water and ethanol. The obtained SiO2@CDs NPs were dispersed in 10 mL of ethanol for later use.

AuNCs were synthesized according to a previous report (Cai et al., 2021). In brief, 2 mL of HAuCl4 solution (10 mg/mL) was added into 8 mL of GSH solution (6.25 mM) with gentle stirring for 5 min and reacted at 70 °C for 24 h. After dialysis, the AuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The slides treated with piranha solution were fixed in a thin layer chromatography (TLC) chamber with SiO2@CDs NPs suspension (0.7 wt%) prepared in the previous step and fixed. Then, the TLC chamber was heated in an oven at 40 °C and PC film was obtained after all the ethanol solvent evaporated. Subsequently, the PC film was immersed in the AuNCs solution for 24 h to adsorb AuNCs. After that, the PC@AuNCs was cleaned with water and dried at 60 °C. Finally, the PC@AuNCs was immersed in CuCl2 solution (20 μM) for 1 h and cleaned with water to obtain the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ film. Above film was dried at 60 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay of TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ were incubated in a series of TC solution with different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 10 μM) for 30 min. After evaporation of water at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In order to evaluate the selectivity, other antibiotics or potential interfering species (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, clarithromycin, chloramphenicol, kanamycin, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, demeclocycline, and doxycycline) were introduced to replace TC. In addition, the fluorescence responses for the mixtures of TC and the above antibiotics or potential interfering species were recorded with the same procedure.

To evaluate the applicability of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ in practical TC detection, milk and honey were purchased from the local supermarket in Tianjin. The milk and honey samples were prepared as the previous reports with slight modifications (Yin & Tong, 2022). For milk samples, 1 mL of milk containing different concentrations of spiked TC (0, 10, 50, and 100 μM) was mixed with 9 mL of trichloroacetic acid (10% w/v) in centrifuge tubes, respectively, and sonicated for 20 min. Thereafter, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for the future use. For honey samples, 100 mg of honey was dissolved in 1 mL of ultrapure water, and then, the different concentration of TC (0, 10, 50, and 100 μM) was added into above solution and diluted into 10 mL for future use.

The morphologies of SiO2 NPs, SiO2@CDs NPs, and AuNCs were observed in TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2A, SiO2@CDs NPs exhibited a spherical morphology with an average diameter of 281.6 nm. Compared with SiO2 NPs (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material), CDs with an average diameter of 3.9 nm were observed on the surface of SiO2@CDs NPs (Fig. 2B and Fig. S2, Supplementary Material), indicating that the CDs were successfully modified on the surface of SiO2 NPs. Elemental mapping images (Fig. S3 Supplementary Material) and the EDS spectrum (Fig. S4, Supplementary Material) illustrated the distribution of C, N, O, and Si of SiO2@CDs NPs. As presented in Fig. 2C, AuNCs were uniformly distributed with an average diameter of 3.2 nm. Dynamic light scattering measurements showed that the hydrodynamic diameters of SiO2@CDs NPs and AuNCs were 295.3 and 3.6 nm, respectively (Fig. S5, Supplementary Material). In addition, the zeta potentials of SiO2@CDs NPs and AuNCs were measured as 7.7 and − 14.2 mV, respectively (Fig. S6, Supplementary Material), demonstrating that the surface of SiO2@CDs NPs was positively charged. Therefore, AuNCs with a negative charge could be adsorbed onto the surface of SiO2@CDs NPs. As shown in Fig. S7 (Supplementary Material), the UV–vis spectra of AuNCs exhibited a distinct absorption process in the UV region, with an onset at 450 nm. Similarly, SiO2@CDs NPs also exhibited broad absorption characteristics. These results demonstrate the successful synthesis of AuNCs and SiO2@CDs NPs. The photoluminescence performance of AuNCs and SiO2@CDs NPs was investigated via fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S8A and Fig. S8B (Supplementary Material), their maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of AuNCs were observed at 412 and 610 nm, respectively. In addition, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of SiO2@CDs NPs were observed at 372 and 456 nm, respectively. Moreover, the absorption spectra of TC and PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ were analyzed to investigate any potential energy transfer mechanism (Jin, Yang, Sun, & Gui, 2021; Huy et al., 2022; Zu et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. S9 (Supplementary Material), the absorption peaks of TC were observed at 275 and 359 nm, which did not overlap with the emission peak of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+. Therefore, no energy transfer occurs between TC and PC@AuNCs/Cu2+.

The morphology of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ was examined via SEM. As shown in Fig. 2D, the SiO2 NPs in PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ were closely arranged and exhibited a typical face-centered cubic structure. Fig. 2E displays the reflectance spectra of the PC, illustrating that the PBG of PC prepared using SiO2 NPs with a diameter of 281.6 nm, was located at 650.8 nm, in accordance with the Bragg diffraction theory. Simultaneously, its red color was easily observed by the naked eye, further confirming the light modulation property of the periodic PC. In addition, elemental mapping images confirmed the presence and distribution of C, O, Si, Cu, and Au (Fig. S10, Supplementary Material). To further evaluate the presence of elements and their valence states, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, characteristic peaks corresponding to C, O, N, S, Si, Cu, and Au were detected, indicating the presence of these elements in the various materials. Furthermore, the XPS spectra of Au 4f revealed the characteristic peaks at 84.4 and 88.1 eV, which could be deconvoluted into a doublet with a lower binding energy (84.1 and 87.8 eV) corresponding to the Au(0) species and another doublet with a higher binding energy (84.7 and 88.3 eV) corresponding to the Au(I) species. This demonstrates the coexistence of Au(0) and Au(I), further confirming the presence of AuNCs on the surface of the PC (Fig. S11A, Supplementary Material). The deconvoluted peaks at 932.2 and 933.9 eV in the XPS spectra of Cu 2p indicated the coexistence of Cu(I) and Cu(II) species in PC@AuNCs/Cu2+, suggesting that 23.18% of Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ by the GSH on the surface of AuNCs (Fig. S11B, Supplementary Material) (Zhao et al., 2021). Moreover, the XPS spectra of C 1 s displayed characteristic peaks at 284.8, 285.3, and 288.0 eV, which were attributed to C–C/C=C, CN, and CO bonds, respectively. This indicates the presence of CDs on SiO2@CDs NPs (Fig. S11C, Supplementary Material). These results collectively confirm that the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensing mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the feasibility and sensing mechanism for the TC sensor, the influence of Cu2+ concentration on fluorescence sensor was first investigated. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of PC@AuNCs at 610 nm gradually decreased with increasing Cu2+ concentration, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 456 nm exhibited no significant change. The fluorescence intensity ratio value of I610/I456 exhibited a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentrations within the range of 0–20 μM (Fig. 3B). Therefore, the Cu2+ concentration of 20 μM was selected to construct the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TC sensing. Meanwhile, the fluorescence enhancement effect of the PC was explored by comparing the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs on bare glass and the PC. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs on the PC was 7.6 times higher than that on bare glass (Fig. 3C). The enhancement effect can be attributed to the slow light effect at the blue edge of the PBG. At the edge of the PBG, the enhanced energy density and prolonged residence time lead to an increase in the DOS. Thus, photons travel with a reduced group velocity, thereby enhancing light–matter interactions and increasing the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs (Li, Li, Wang, & Yu, 2021). The enhanced fluorescence intensity could provide higher sensitivity for the subsequent TC detection. Subsequently, the fluorescence response of TC by the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor was implemented to evaluate the feasibility of TC detection. After immersing PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ in a TC solution for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of PC@AuNCs at 610 nm significantly increased, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 456 nm remained unchanged, indicating that the quenching effect of Cu2+ decreased. The reason for the removal of Cu2+ could be attributed to the β-diketone configuration of TC, which could coordinate with Cu2+ in the sensor to form a stable complex, resulting in the dissociation of Cu2+ and the recovery of fluorescent response from PC@AuNCs (Sang et al., 2022).

The fluorescence enhancement and quenching mechanism was further investigated using the fluorescence lifetime of PC@AuNCs in the presence or absence of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs on PC (11.68 μs) was longer than that on bare glass (10.46 μs), indicating that the light–matter interaction was enhanced via the increased DOS and slow light effect resulting from the PC. Upon immersion of PC@AuNCs in Cu2+ solution, the fluorescence lifetime of PC@AuNCs was significantly shortened from 11.68 to 8.89 μs, demonstrating that the quenching process of AuNCs could be attributed to dynamic quenching. The quenching process was presumably due to electron transfer between AuNCs and Cu2+. In contrast, the fluorescence lifetime of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ was restored to 11.63 μs after PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ was immersed in TC solution, which was close to the fluorescence lifetime of PC@AuNCs before the addition of Cu2+, indicating that the Cu2+ in the sensor was removed by TC.

The prepared PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensors were immersed in a series of TC solutions with different concentrations to evaluate the analytical performance of the sensors for TC detection. As shown in Fig. 4A, with increasing TC concentration, the fluorescence intensity of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ at 610 nm gradually increased owing to the removal of Cu2+, whereas the fluorescence at 456 nm remained unchanged. As a result, the fluorescence intensity ratio value of I610/I456 exhibited a good linear relationship with TC concentrations within the range of 0.1–10 μM (Fig. 4B). The regression equation was I610/I456 = 0.41161[TC] + 0.4385, demonstrating a good linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9919). The limit of detection was 34 nM, which was significantly below the MRLs of TC regulated by the U.S. FDA (676 nM) and the EU (225 nM). In addition, compared with previously reported methods (Table S1, Supplementary Material), the proposed strategy exhibited a comparable or lower detection limit. It displayed a sensitive and simple quantitative detection potential without requiring special expensive equipment, benefiting from the fluorescence enhancement effect of the PC. Furthermore, the fluorescence color of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ ratiometric fluorescence sensor gradually changed from blue to bright orange with the increase in TC concentration (Fig. 4C), enabling on-site visual detection with the naked eye. Moreover, the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different TC concentrations were calculated. As shown in Fig. 4D, the sensing system exhibited blue fluorescence with CIE coordinates of (0.266, 0.256) before the addition of TC. As the TC concentration increased from 0.1 to 10 μM, an obvious change from blue to pink (0.316, 0.291), and ultimately to orange, was observed, along with CIE coordinates shifting from (0.266, 0.256) to (0.471, 0.399), demonstrating the potential of the sensing system for TC visual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference properties of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor toward TC
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor, several potential coexisting cations and anions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −, and SO4 2−), as well as antibiotics (clarithromycin, chloramphenicol, kanamycin, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, demeclocycline, and doxycycline), were selected. As shown in Fig. 5A and Fig. S12 (Supplementary Material), none of these coexisting substances had a significant effect on the fluorescence of the sensor except for the TC family (TCs). Only TCs could enhance the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm because of their similar chemical structures, resulting in an increase in the I610/I456 ratio, with TC showing the most pronounced response. In addition, the anti-interference performance of the ratiometric sensor was investigated based on the mixture of the above interfering substances and TC. As shown in Fig. 5B, there was a negligible influence on the TC detection properties of PC@AuNCs/Cu2+, when these interfering substances were present. These results indicate that the sensing system possesses good anti-interference properties and has the potential for the assay of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility and application performance of the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor in practical detection, the standard addition method was used to analyze TC in milk and honey samples. Various concentrations of TC standard solutions were added to pretreated milk and honey samples for determination according to the method described in materials and methods section. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of TC ranged satisfactorily from 91.62% to 101.09% for milk and honey samples with different spiked concentrations (0–10 μM). In addition, the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 3) of the TC sensor were less than 4.65% for all samples. Moreover, compared with previously reported methods (Table S2, Supplementary Material), the proposed method exhibited acceptable recoveries and RSDs. Therefore, these results verify that the PC@AuNCs/Cu2+ sensor demonstrates outstanding accuracy and reliability, indicating high potential for further use in food safety applications.

In summary, a dual-emission PC ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully fabricated using a simple self-assembly and adsorption process for sensitive detection of TC. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs was enhanced by the PC with a specific PBG through the increased DOS and the slow light effect, thereby providing higher sensitivity for TC sensing. The fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched with the addition of Cu2+ and restored owing to the complexation of TC with Cu2+, whereas the fluorescence of CDs as a reference signal remained unchanged. This resulted in the variation in the fluorescence ratio I610/I456, which is related to TC concentration. The fluorescence ratio exhibited a good linear relationship with the TC concentration within a certain concentration range, with a low detection limit of 34 nM, comparable with or superior to previous reports. In addition, it revealed a distinct fluorescence color variation from blue to orange. Furthermore, the sensor effectively determined the concentrations of TC in milk and honey samples, achieving satisfactory recoveries. This study presents a novel strategy for rapid TC detection and demonstrates potential for practical application in food safety monitoring.

Licheng Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Xiaowen Jiang: Validation, Investigation. Yijun Li: Resources, Formal analysis. Xiwen He: Validation, Supervision. Langxing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yukui Zhang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074067).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139418.
